coredata.dc:title: Coal-based carbon quantum dots modified Ag3PO4/Ni-MOF composites enhanced visible light photocatalytic degradation of phenol
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yijun
Li, Mei
Jin, Zhiliang
Xu, Shiyong
Li, Ziyu
Ding, Meijuan
coredata.dc:description: Coal tar pitch-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were meticulously prepared via ball milling. Ag3PO4/Ni-MOF/CQDs composites were synthesized through an in-situ precipitation method, and their performance as composite catalysts for the photocatalytic degradation of phenol was thoroughly investigated. Among these composites, Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 emerged as the most effective catalyst, achieving a remarkable degradation rate of 98% for 10 mg/L phenol within just 30 min, with a corresponding rate constant of 0.0620 min−1. Notably, the photocatalytic degradation of phenol exhibited robust activity across a spectrum of pH levels, different water matrices, and varying concentrations of anions (SO4 2−, Cl−, CO3 2−, HPO4 2− and HCO3 −). Furthermore, after undergoing 4 cycles of reaction, the catalyst demonstrated commendable stability. The outcomes of free radical capture experiments revealed that the primary active species involved in the degradation process were ·OH and h +. The electron transport pathways were analyzed based on the band gap structure of the catalyst, and a possible degradation mechanism was proposed. Upon the combination of Ni-MOF and Ag3PO4, the alteration of metal-O bonds facilitated the formation of a tightly bonded interface, thereby establishing a Z-scheme heterojunction. The introduction of CQDs as an electron transport medium accelerated electron transfer, mitigated the recombination of photogenerated charges, and thereby enhanced the photocatalytic performance of the heterostructure.

head.ce:title: Coal-based carbon quantum dots modified Ag3PO4/Ni-MOF composites enhanced visible light photocatalytic degradation of phenol

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Ningxia Key Laboratory of Solar Chemical conversion technology, State Key Laboratory of National Ethnic Affairs Commission Chemical Technology, North Minzu University, Yinchuan 750021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Ningxia Key Laboratory of Solar Chemical conversion technology, State Key Laboratory of National Ethnic Affairs Commission Chemical Technology, North Minzu University, Yinchuan 750021, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Coal tar pitch-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were meticulously prepared via ball milling. Ag3PO4/Ni-MOF/CQDs composites were synthesized through an in-situ precipitation method, and their performance as composite catalysts for the photocatalytic degradation of phenol was thoroughly investigated. Among these composites, Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 emerged as the most effective catalyst, achieving a remarkable degradation rate of 98% for 10 mg/L phenol within just 30 min, with a corresponding rate constant of 0.0620 min−1. Notably, the photocatalytic degradation of phenol exhibited robust activity across a spectrum of pH levels, different water matrices, and varying concentrations of anions (SO4 2−, Cl−, CO3 2−, HPO4 2− and HCO3 −). Furthermore, after undergoing 4 cycles of reaction, the catalyst demonstrated commendable stability. The outcomes of free radical capture experiments revealed that the primary active species involved in the degradation process were ·OH and h +. The electron transport pathways were analyzed based on the band gap structure of the catalyst, and a possible degradation mechanism was proposed. Upon the combination of Ni-MOF and Ag3PO4, the alteration of metal-O bonds facilitated the formation of a tightly bonded interface, thereby establishing a Z-scheme heterojunction. The introduction of CQDs as an electron transport medium accelerated electron transfer, mitigated the recombination of photogenerated charges, and thereby enhanced the photocatalytic performance of the heterostructure.

In this study, coal tar asphalt-based CQDs were prepared, and Ag3PO4/Ni-MOF/CQDs were synthesized by in-situ deposition. The degradation rate constant of Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 composite catalyst for phenol is 0.0620 min−1, which is 51.7 times that of CQDs and Ni-MOF, and 2.3 times that of Ag3PO4, with excellent degradation activity and good cycling stability. According to the UV–Vis DRS and Mott-Schottky test results, the band structure of the catalyst was calculated. The conduction and valence band positions of Ag3PO4 were 0.47 eV and 2.89 eV, respectively, and the conduction band positions of Ni-MOF were -0.78 eV and 2.35 eV, respectively. Ag3PO4 and Ni-MOF form a Z-scheme heterojunction, and CQDs provide a possible electron transport path as an electron transport medium. Capture experiments and ESR tests showed that holes and hydroxyl radicals played a major role in the photocatalytic degradation of phenol by Ag3PO4/Ni-MOF/CQDs, and finally phenol was degraded into H2O and CO2.

ce:list-item.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Phenolic pollutants primarily originate from activities such as fossil fuel refining, coking, pharmaceutical production, plastic industry, etc., representing a widespread category of organic contaminants [1]. Among them, phenol stands out as a well-known non-volatile phenol with a special odor, which is classified as three types of carcinogens [2,3]. Due to its aromatic nature, phenol presents challenges in degradation. In addition, because the phenol structure contains hydroxyl functional groups, its solubility in water is high, and it has persistent biological toxicity [4,5]. Consequently, phenol in wastewater needs to be deeply treated to reduce its concentration in wastewater to 0.1–1 mg/L [6]. It is difficult to degrade by traditional biotechnology [7] and chemical oxidation [8], thus necessitating the development of an efficient and sustainable approach for its remove from wastewater. Photocatalytic degradation of phenolic compounds, as advanced oxidation process (AOPs) [9,10], has the advantages of high efficiency in degrading pollutants under visible light, does not cause secondary pollution [11,12], and has a wide range of light resources, which makes photocatalysis widely used to degrade pollutants. The photocatalytic process involves utilizing light to stimulate the catalyst to generate electric charges to engage in redox reaction [13,14]. While the holes remain within the relatively stable valence band, the electrons converge on the conduction band (CB) of the catalyst, synergistically reacting with the solution to produce superoxide radicals (·O2 −) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH) [15,16], effectively facilitating contaminant removal.

The application of semiconductor nanomaterials in field of photocatalysis has garnered significant attention from numerous researchers, such as TiO2 [17], NiO [18], Ag3PO4 [19] and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [20]. These catalysts exhibit promising potential for the photocatalysis degradation of persistent organic pollutants in water. Among them, the semiconductor material Ag3PO4 possesses a narrow band gap of approximately 2.4 eV, robust absorption in the visible light region, exceptional photooxidation capability, and high quantum efficiency (90%) [21–23]. These attributes render Ag3PO4 an effective photocatalytic degradation catalyst. However, Ag3PO4 also presents notable drawbacks, including a small specific surface area, large particle diameter, high cost, and susceptibility to photocorrosion, which can compromise catalyst stability [19,24,25]. These shortcomings can be overcome by constructing heterojunctions with other materials [26]. The Z-scheme heterojunction is a common heterostructure with a small band gap and a large overpotential, which can better suppress the recombination of electron-hole pairs [27].

MOFs are coordination polymers self-assembled by organic ligands containing C, O, N and other non-metallic elements alongside transition metal ions [28,29]. At present, a wide array of MOFs has been synthesized, with extensive investigations conducted on their applications due to their advantages, such as large specific surface area, controllable structure, high porosity, and unique morphology [30,31]. Among the diverse range of MOFs, Ni-MOF stands out for its non-toxicity, low cost, and diverse morphology [32], finding widespread application in gas adsorption [33] and electrocatalysis [34]. The multi-level structure of Ni-MOF provides favorable conditions for photocatalytic reactions [35]. However, limited research exists in the realm of photocatalytic degradation, likely due to its low photocatalytic activity [36]. Combining Ni-MOF with other semiconductor materials to construct heterojunctions can enhance its photocatalytic activity.

CQDs are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials boasting high conductivity, stability, excellent optical properties, and unique electronic properties [37,38]. Numerous catalysts based on CQDs have been synthesized, and extensive studies have been conducted in the field of photocatalytic degradation [39]. The advantages of preparing CQDs from coal tar pitch are as follows: (i) Low cost and large storage capacity of carbon source. (ii) High-value utilization of asphalt can be achieved. (iii) The preparation method does not use strong acid and alkali, which is relatively green and pollution-free. The conjugated π structure of CQDs enables them to act as electron acceptors or donors to accelerate electron transfer, effectively suppress photo-induced charge recombination, and enhancing solar energy conversion efficiency [40,41]. Leveraging these properties, CQDs can be utilized to modify catalyst and broaden the light absorption region of the composite catalyst [42]. The aromatic ring in CQDs can form a π-π stacking structure with the aromatic rings in phenolic compounds, which can degrade phenolic pollutants more efficiently [43].

In this study, a novel composite catalyst was synthesized. During the Ag3PO4 crystal formation process, Ni-MOF and CQDs were introduced, yielding the first-ever synthesis of an Ag3PO4/Ni-MOF heterojunction incorporating coal-based CQDs as electronic mediators. The photocatalytic performance of Ag3PO4/Ni-MOF/CQDs for phenol degradation was investigated.

The experimental coal tar pitch comes from Ningdong Energy Chemical Industry Base. The reagents required for the experiment were purchased from Aladdin Company, mainly including: phenol (C6H5OH), silver nitrate (AgNO3), ammonia (NH3·H2O), nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·H2O), disodium hydrogen phosphate dodecahydrate (Na2HPO4·12H2O), 2-methylimidazole (C4H6N2), sodium formate (HCOONa), tert‑butyl alcohol ((CH3)3COH), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30%), ammonium oxalate ((NH4)2C2O4), reagents were analytically pure.

Coal-based CQDs were prepared by ball milling assisted H2O2 oxidation. Initially, the coal tar pitch asphalt underwent freezing and subsequent crushing using a law crusher. Due to the large particle size, the coal tar pitch was further ground to 180 mesh using a mortar. The coal tar pitch powder 3.0 g was mixed with 30 ml H2O and then ball milled. The ball mill was operated at 500 r/min, with cycles consisting of 1 h of operation, followed by a 10 min suspension and reverse operation for 1 h, repeated for a total of 5 cycles. Following ball milling, 20 mL of 30% H2O2 solution was added to the solid-liquid mixture, which underwent magnetic stirring at room temperature for 24 h. The solution was then filtered and washed until transparent and free of impurities, followed by evaporation and concentration to about 10 ml at 80 °C. After dialysis and purification for 24 h, it was freeze-dried at −40 °C to obtain CQDs solid powder. The preparation process is illustrated in Fig. 1 (a).

The 2.37 g NiCl2·H2O was dissolved in 60 mL H2O, 0.08 g 2-methylimidazole and 0.07 g HCOONa were dissolved in the above solution, stirred magnetically for 30 min, ultrasonically dispersed for 10 min, and moved into a 100 mL reaction kettle, kept warm at 120 °C in an oven for 24 h, which washed three times with ethanol and water, after drying at 60 °C for 4 h, the light green Ni-MOF powder was collected. The preparation method is given in Fig. 1(b).

Ag3PO4 was prepared via the coprecipitation method. Specifically, 0.51 g of AgNO3 was dissolved in 70 mL H2O and 10% NH3·H2O was added to form solution A. Meanwhile, 0.52 g of Na2HPO4·12H2O was dissolved in 70 mL H2O to form solution B. Solution B was added to solution A drop by drop, after 10 h of magnetic stirring in the dark, and the solid was extracted using a vacuum filter. Washed three times with H2O and ethanol, and dried at 60 °C in an oven for 4 h to obtain yellow-green Ag3PO4 powder.

The composite catalyst Ag3PO4/Ni-MOF/CQDs was synthesized via the in-situ deposition method. The prepared Ni-MOF powder was dissolved in 70 mL H2O. After ultrasonication to complete dispersion. Subsequently, 0.52 g Na2HPO4·12H2O was added and stirred to dissolve it. The mass fraction of Ni-MOF in Ag3PO4 was x% (x = 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 50, 70, 90 and 100). Additionally, 0.51 g AgNO3 was dissolved in 70 mL CQDs solution and dispersed by ultrasound for 10 min. The mass fraction of CQDs in Ag3PO4 was y% (y = 0.05, 0.15, 0.30, 0.45 and 0.60). The subsequent steps followed the same as the preparation process of Ag3PO4. The sample was designated Ag-Ni-x/CQDs-y. The preparation method is plotted in Fig. 1(c).

The adsorption-desorption equilibrium was achieved by adding 20 mg photocatalyst into 30 mL of a phenol solution with the concentration of 10 mg/L, followed by stirring in dark field for 30 min. After sampling, the mixture was transferred to a reactor for photoreaction under a 10 W LED lamp (λ ≥ 420 nm) with 1 mL samples collected every 15 min. Separation of the phenol solution and photocatalyst was accomplished using a 0.22 μm filter membrane. The concentration of the samples was determined through high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) with a column temperature set at 30 °C and detection wavelength 270 nm. The mobile phase consisted of methanol-water with a volume ratio of 70: 30, flowing at a flow rate of 1 mL min−1, with an injection was 20 μL. For the photocatalyst stability test, the sample underwent washing with ethanol and ultra-pure water, followed by filtration and drying. The collected sample was then recycled using the aforementioned method. The phenol degradation rate is calculated using Eq. (1), while the pseudo-first-order kinetics of the catalyst was fitted using Eq. (2).

In order to explore the photoelectrochemical activities of catalysts, they were tested on the CHI 760E electrochemical workstation. The AC frequency of the Mott-Schottky curve (M-S, −1 V to 0 V vs. SCE) was 1500 Hz. The light source of the transient photocurrent (I-T, 0.2 potential vs. SCE) was a 300 W xenon lamp with a 420 nm filter. The scanning speed of the linear scanning voltammetry curve (LSV, −0.6 V to 0.2 V vs. SCE) and alternating impedance spectroscopy (EIS, 0.2 potential vs. SCE) was 10 mV/s. The test conditions used in this study are as follows: 0.2 mol/L Na2SO4 is used as the electrolyte solution, and the reference electrode (saturated calomel electrode), the counter electrode (platinum electrode) and the working electrode are standard three electrodes. Working electrode preparation: the 5 mg of catalyst was weighed, sonicated in a mixed solution of Nafion and ethanol at a volume ratio of 9: 1, and uniformly coated on 1 × 1 cm2 ITO conductive glass.

The morphology and structure of the samples were photographed by electron microscope (SEM, TEM and HRTEM, JEOL JEM-F200, Japan) with 200 kV accelerating voltage, and the composite catalyst was scanned by energy spectrum (JED-2300T). The crystal structures of different photocatalysts were characterized through X-ray diffraction (XRD, RINT-2000) at a scan rate of 8 °/min. The elemental composition and chemical state of different catalysts were characterized through X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ESCALAB Xi+). The energy of the full spectrum and the fine spectrum were 100 eV and 20 eV, respectively. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherm of the catalysts was measured through a solid surface analyzer (ASAP2020M), and the degassing condition was vacuuming at 150 °C for 8 h. The photoluminescence spectrometer (PL, FluoroMAX-4) was used to test the fluorescence spectrum and lifetime of the photocatalyst. The active free radicals during the degradation reaction were tested through electron paramagnetic resonance (ESR, Bruker EMXplus-6/1) under dark field and 300 W xenon lamp illumination for 5 min, respectively.

Morphology and structure analysis of the various catalysts were conducted using SEM, TEM, and HRTEM, as exhibited in Fig. 2 . The SEM image of Ag3PO4 is given in Fig. 2(a), revealing irregularly spherical coarse nanoparticle [22]. As tested in Fig. 2(b), Ni-MOF exhibits a spherical nanoflower produced by the stacking of nanosheets [45]. Fig. 2(c) and (d) display the SEM images of Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45, where Ag3PO4 nanoparticles are clearly observed visible, although only portion of the nanoflower structure is discernible in Fig. 2(d). This partial observation may be attributed to the structural alterations of Ni-MOF during the preparation process, leading to dispersion and integration of some nanosheets with the Ag3PO4 particles.

The TEM images of Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 are given in Fig. 2(e) and (f), revealing the composite of Ag3PO4 and Ni-MOF nanosheets. The HRTEM images in Fig. 2(g) displays distinct lattice fringes, with the red-marked portion representing CQDs. The lattice fringes are obvious, with a lattice spacing is 0.205 nm corresponding to (100) crystal plane of CQDs [46]. The lattice spacing of Ag3PO4 measures 0.265 nm, corresponding to the Ag3PO4 (210) plane [47], consistent with the XRD results. The elemental mapping of the main elements contained in the Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 composite catalyst is plotted in Fig. 2(h), showcasing even distribution of the six elements (Ag, Ni, C, O, P and S) within the catalyst. The EDX scan results are presented in Fig. 2(i), validate the elemental mapping findings, confirming the successful preparation of Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 composites.

Through testing the XRD spectra of Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45, Ag3PO4 and Ni-MOF, the crystal phase structure of catalyst was obtained, as illustrated in Fig. 3 (a). The most prominent diffraction peak corresponding to Ag3PO4 (210) was observed at 2 θ = 33.20°, with additional main diffraction peaks aligning with the standard card (JCPDS No.06–0505), indicating of its body-centered cubic phase structure [48]. Ni-MOF appeared amorphous, with a distinct diffraction peak at 11.02°, exhibiting a slight shift to 11.51° in the composite catalyst, demonstrating a strong coupling between Ag3PO4 and Ni-MOF [29]. These findings, coupled with the results of morphology analysis, affirm the successfully recombined of Ag3PO4 and Ni-MOF.

The molecular structures of Ag3PO4, CQDs, Ni-MOF and Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 were tested through FT-IR spectrometer, as depicted in Fig. 3(b). The peak observed at 1076 cm−1 in the spectrum of Ag3PO4 corresponds to the stretching vibration mode of the P-O bond in PO4 3− [49]. Additionally, the peak at 864 cm−1 represents the symmetrical and asymmetric stretching vibration of the P-O-P bond, while the peak at 551 cm−1 corresponds to the bending vibration mode of the O=P-O bond [21]. The peaks of CQDs at 1668, 1429, 1107, 1037, and 530 cm−1, attributed to C=C, -CH3/-CH2, C-N, C-O-C, and S-S, respectively [44,50]. The infrared spectra of Ni-MOF revealed characterized by the telescopic vibration of the C-H bond in the imidazole ring at 2968 cm−1 and 2891 cm−1 [45], while peaks at 1381 cm−1 and 1609 cm−1 were assigned to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration modes of -COO− [32]. Peaks at 1283 cm−1 and 1040 cm−1 belonged to the in-plane bending vibration of the 2-methylimidazole ring in Ni-MOF [51]. Notably, only one of the peaks was observed in the infrared spectrum of the composite catalyst, indicating structural changes in Ni-MOF during the recombination process. The wide absorption band in the range of 3032–3076 cm−1 is incriminated to the OH/NH bond [25]. In the spectrum of Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45, characteristic peaks of Ag3PO4 and CQDs displayed a slight blue shift, whereas those of Ni-MOF exhibited a slight red shift, confirming interaction between the catalysts [47].

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of the catalyst are presented in Fig. 3(c) and (d), while the adsorption characteristic parameters of Ni-MOF, Ag3PO4, Ag-Ni-70 and Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 are exhibited in Table 1 . The results reveal that the isotherms of the various catalysts exhibit a IV (a) behavior, with the presence of H3 hysteresis loop confirming their mesoporous structure [52]. Specifically, the specific surface areas of Ni-MOF, Ag3PO4, Ag-Ni-70 and Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 were calculated to be 32.5 m²/g, 6.2 m²/g, 64.8 m²/g and 66.1 m²/g, respectively. The combination of Ni-MOF and Ag3PO4 led to a doubling of the SBET, and subsequent surface modification with CQDs further increased the SBET of Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45, thereby enhancing the reactivity of the composite material.

The surface energy states and elemental composition of Ag3PO4, CQDs, Ni-MOF and Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 were analyzed through XPS analysis. The XPS spectrum is presented in Fig. 4 (a), illustrating the main elements present in the Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 catalyst, including C, Ag, Ni, O, P and S, consistent with the TEM element mapping results. Fig. 4(b) displays the C 1 s spectrum of the catalyst, revealing three distinct peaks. The peak attributed to the CC and C=C bond is located at 284.80 eV, while the peaks of CQDs at binding energies of 286.10 eV and 288.45 eV correspond to the COH and the OC=O bonds, respectively [36,44]. Notably, in the composite catalyst, the peak of CQDs shifts to higher binding energy, while the peak of Ni-MOF shifts to lower binding energy.

Fig. 4(c) and (f) illustrate the Ag 3d and P 2p spectra of Ag3PO4 and Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 composite catalysts. In Ag3PO4, the Ag 3d spectrum exhibits two peaks at 367.75 eV and 373.75 eV, corresponding to the 3d5/2 and 3d3/2 orbitals of the Ag+ electronic state, respectively [53], while the peak of P 2p is at 132.90 eV. Conversely, in the composite catalyst Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45, the peaks associated with Ag+ and P5+ shift to higher binding energies, suggesting a decrease in the electron cloud density of Ag3PO4 after the composite formation with Ni-MOF and CQDs [54].

Fig. 4(d) displays the Ni 2p spectrum of Ni-MOF and Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45, revealing four distinct peaks. The two main peaks at 856.05 eV and 873.70 eV correspond to the Ni 2p3/2 and Ni 2p1/2 orbitals of Ni2+, respectively, while the peaks at 861.70 eV and 879.45 eV being satellite peaks in the 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 orbits [36,55]. In the Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 composite catalyst, all four Ni2+ peaks shift to lower binding energies, indicating an increase in the electron density of Ni-MOF [54].

The O 1 s spectra of Ag3PO4, CQDs, Ni-MOF and Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 are plotted in Fig. 4(e). In Ag3PO4, two peaks were fitted, with the peak at 530.70 eV attributed to Ag-O bond, and the peak at 531.80 eV associated with the -OH group [49]. Similarly, Ni-MOF exhibited two peaks, with binding energies of 533.50 eV and 531.65 eV corresponding to the -COOH group and ONi bond, respectively [56]. The presence of two types of metal-O bonds demonstrates the existence of bimetallic active sites, and the metal neutrality as active sites can significantly enhance the performance of the catalyst [57]. CQDs displayed a single peak at 531.35 eV, attributed to the CO bond of the sp2 carbon site [58]. Notably, in Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45, this peak shifted to higher binding energy. Interestingly, no peak was detected in the Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 composite catalyst, except at 531.55 eV, indicating changes in both the Ag-O and ONi bonds during catalyst synthesis. This suggests strong interface interaction between the catalysts [2], thereby enhancing the catalyst's activity in the reaction [59].

The results of PL and TRPL test for Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45, Ag3PO4, Ni-MOF and CQDs are presented in Fig. 5 . As depicted in Fig. 5(a), it is evident that Ag3PO4 exhibits the highest fluorescence intensity, while the Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 composite catalyst shows the lowest intensity. This observation suggests that the composite catalyst has a reduced photogenerated charge recombination efficiency [60]. CQDs are known for their role as electron transport media [41], and their incorporation into the composite with Ag3PO4 and Ni-MOF helps inhibit the recombination of electron-hole pairs.

The TRPL attenuation spectra of the different catalysts are shown in Fig. 5(b), with decay kinetics are fitted according to Eq. (3) and the average fluorescence lifetime of the catalysts is calculated according to Eq. (4).

As depicted in Table 2 , the composite catalysts exhibit varying average lifetime, with τave = 0.038 ns for Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45, τave = 0.037 ns for Ag3PO4, τave = 0.019 ns for Ni-MOF, and τave = 0.024 ns for CQDs. Consequently, the Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 composite demonstrates the longest average lifetime, indicating the highest photogenerated charge transfer rate [61]. The synergistic effects between Ag3PO4 and Ni-MOF, coupled with the contribution of CQDs, facilitate the efficient separation and transfer of electrons-holes pairs.

It is well known that the transfers and migration efficiency of electron–hole charge carriers play a crucial role in photocatalytic applications [62]. The photoelectrochemical properties of Ag3PO4, Ni-MOF, Ag-Ni-70 and Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 were comprehensively characterized. Fig. 6 (a) shows the I-T test results after 4 irradiation cycles reveal a distinct order of photocurrent response intensity: Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 > Ag-Ni-70 > Ni-MOF > Ag3PO4. This hierarchy suggests that the combination of Ni-MOF and Ag3PO4 increases the electron transfer rate [36], further accelerated by the introduction of CQDs. In addition, the transient photocurrent of all photocatalysts is reversible and reproducible, which proves that the catalyst has good photoelectrochemical stability [57]. The LSV of different catalysts, presented in Fig. 6(b), where the current density of Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 is significantly higher than that of Ag-Ni-70, Ni-MOF and Ag3PO4. This indicates a superior charge transfer rate and stronger ability to inhibit electron-hole recombination [63]. Fig. 6(c) presents the EIS test results, with the radius of curvature arranged in the following order: Ni-MOF > Ag3PO4 > Ag-Ni-70 > Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45. This suggests that Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 exhibits the lowest resistivity for charge transfer and the most robust separation ability for photogenerated charges [64]. This means that the incorporation of CQDs relatively increases the conductivity of nanomaterials, thereby improving the formation of photocarriers and charge transfer rate [65]. In summary, the comprehensive test results demonstrate that the composite catalyst Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 exhibits the best photoelectrochemical performance.

The visible light adsorption properties of different catalysts were assessed through UV–Vis DRS characterization of Ag3PO4, Ni-MOF, Ag-Ni-70 and Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45. As depicted in Fig. 7 (a), the catalysts exhibit favorable light absorption performance, with Ni-MOF displaying absorption characteristics consistent with its intrinsic properties [36], while the absorption edges of other catalysts appear around 500 nm. Upon combining Ag3PO4 with Ni-MOF, it became evident that Ag-Ni-70 had higher absorbance, indicative of a robust interaction between the two components [48]. The incorporation of CQDs further augmented the light adsorption of Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45, highlighting the significant enhancement in optical performance attributed to CQDs serving as electron transport media [66]. Consequently, the Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 composite catalyst can effectively harness light energy to degrade pollutants more efficiently. The bandgap energy (Eg) values of Ag3PO4 and Ni-MOF were calculated using the Kulblka-Munk function, resulting in 2.42 eV and 3.13 eV, respectively, as given in Fig. 7(b) and (c), with the calculation Eq. As plotted in Fig. 7(d) and (e), the M-S curves of Ag3PO4 and Ni-MOF were measured, from which the flat band edge potential (Efb) of the materials was determined. The Efb values of Ag3PO4 and Ni-MOF were found to be 0.43 V and −0.82 V, respectively. Given their positive slopes, both are classified as n-type semiconductors [15,26]. The Efb of the semiconductor approximates the potential of the catalyst's CB (ECB), which can be calculated according to Eq. (6).

The ECB values of Ag3PO4 and Ni-MOF were determined to be 0.47 eV and −0.78 eV, respectively. Similarly, the valence band edge potential (EVB) of the catalysts was calculated according to Eq. (7).

The calculated EVB values for Ag3PO4 and Ni-MOF are 2.89 eV and 2.35 eV, respectively. The band gap structure of the catalyst system is presented in Fig. 7(f). Considering the XPS characterization results, it can be concluded that the Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 composite catalyst forms a Z-scheme heterojunction [50].

The photocatalytic activity for phenol degradation was investigated in the absence of catalyst and with various catalysts. As depicted in Fig. 8 (a), minimal degradation of phenol solution occurred under visible light without catalyst addition. When only pure CQDs or Ni-MOF were employed in the reaction, negligible phenol removal was observed. Elemental Ag3PO4, however, exhibited the capability to degrade 89% of phenol within 60 min. After Ag3PO4 and Ni-MOF were combined, the degradation rate of Ag-Ni-70 towards phenol reached 96% within 60 min. Following the introduction of CQDs, the degradation rates of Ni-MOF/CQDs, Ag3PO4/CQDs and Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 towards phenol increased to 16%, 93% and 99%, respectively, within 60 min. Remarkably, the degradation rate of phenol by Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 reached 98% after just 30 min of reaction, demonstrating the catalytic enhancement achieved through CQDs. Total organic carbon(TOC) analysis showed that the removal rate of TOC reached 98.3%. As given in Fig. 8(b), the rate constant of phenol for Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 was calculated to be 0.0620 min−1, which is 51.7 times higher than that of elemental CQDs (0.0012 min−1) and Ni-MOF (0.0012 min−1), and 2.3 times higher that of Ag3PO4 (0.0270 min−1).

To optimize the catalytic system's degradation efficiency, the optimal ratio of composite catalysts was explored, as presented in Fig. 8(c). Binary composite catalysts comprising Ni-MOF and Ag3PO4 were prepared with mass fractions of 5%, 10%, 15%, 20%, 30%, 50%, 70%, 90% and 100% were prepared to degrade 30 mL of phenol solution with a concentration of 10 mg/L under the same conditions. When the mass fraction of Ni-MOF in Ag3PO4 was 70%, the degradation efficiency of phenol solution was maximized, achieving a degradation rate of approximately 93% when the light reaction was 60 min. When the ratio of Ni-MOF is low, the degradation efficiency of the catalyst cannot be effectively improved, and when the ratio is too high, too many Ni-MOF nanosheets lead to a decrease in catalyst activity [67].

Building upon the experimental findings, different concentrations of CQDs solution were incorporated with Ag-Ni-70, constituting 0.05%, 0.15%, 0.30%, 0.45% and 0.60% of the mass fraction of Ag3PO4, respectively, for the photocatalytic degradation of phenol. The experimental conditions remained consistent to explore the optimal dosage of CQDs, and the experimental results are shown in Fig. 8(d). Introduction of CQDs resulted in 99% phenol degradation within 45 min of reaction. Notably, when the mass fraction of CQDs accounted for 0.45% of Ag3PO4, a degradation rate of phenol could reach 91% within 15 min, while the degradation rate of phenol within 15 min of other composite catalysts was less than 87%. When the loading of CQDs is too small, the electrons cannot be transmitted in time, resulting in a slightly lower degradation efficiency of the catalyst. However, when CQDs accumulate excessively on the surface of the catalyst, it will also hinder charge transfer and may obscure some active sites, which reduces the degradation efficiency of the composite catalyst [19]. Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 emerged as the optimal catalyst ratio, facilitating efficient phenol degradation.

The influence of various factors on the photocatalytic degradation of phenol activity by the Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 composite catalyst was explored, as plotted in Fig. 9 . Fig. 9(a) illustrates the impact of different phenol concentrations on photocatalytic degradation. phenol solution with concentrations of 5, 10, 20, 30 and 40 mg/L were each treated with 0.02 g of Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 composite catalyst for photocatalytic degradation experiments. Following the adsorption-desorption equilibrium in the dark, a slight increase in phenol adsorption on the catalyst was observed with increasing concentration, primarily through visible light catalytic degradation. Phenol degradation rates exceeding 97% within 60 min were achieved at concentrations below 20 mg/L. At 30 and 40 mg/L concentrations, degradation rates of 82% and 76% were attained, respectively. While the phenol degradation rate decreased with increasing concentration, satisfactory degradation rates were still achievable.

To examine the effect of catalyst dosage on the degradation reaction, 0.005, 0.01, 0.02, 0.03 and 0.04 g Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 composites were added to 30 mL of 10 mg/L phenol for photocatalytic experiments. As presented in Fig. 9(b), the sorption of 10 mg/L phenol solution is almost identical for different catalyst contents. Addition of 0.005 g of catalyst resulted in only a 33% degradation rate after 30 min of light exposure, while 0.01 g yielded a 79% degradation rate could reach 79% within the same timeframe. Catalyst additions of 0.02 g and above achieved over 98% degradation within 30 min of light exposure, with marginal degradation rate changes upon further reaction extension. Considering catalyst utilization, 0.02 g of Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 was utilized for phenol solution degradation at a concentration of 10 mg/L to explore other influencing factors.

The pH plays a crucial role in pollutant removal and the catalyst surface charge [4]. Therefore, the effect of solution pH (3, 5, 7, 9 and 11) on photocatalytic degradation activity was investigated using 0.02 g Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45. According to the results of Fig. 9(c), phenol degradation rates at pH = 3, 5, 9, and 11 were 78%, 94%, 99%, and 51% respectively, after 60 min, while at pH = 7, degradation reached 99% within 30 min. Hence, the optimal degradation environment is neutral as both acidic and alkaline conditions inhibit phenol degradation. This is because phenol is a weak acidic substance, and there will be excessive h+ in the acidic environment, which will react with the electrons and holes on the surface of the catalyst. There is excessive OH− in the alkaline environment, which reacts with ·OH, resulting in a decrease in the concentration of active substances in the reaction system, resulting in a decrease in photocatalytic activity [68].

The degradation of phenol in actual water may have different effects [69], so the effects of different water matrices on the degradation of phenol by catalysts were investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. 9(d), encompassing deionized water, natural lake water, and tap water. In addition, the effects of different water matrices on the degradation of phenol by catalysts were also investigated, and the results are presented in Fig. 9(d), followed by deionized water, lake water, and tap water. It was found that the degradation effect of Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 composites on phenol was stronger in natural lake water, which may be due to the fact that a small quantity of inorganic salts contained in lake water increased the degradation rate of phenol, while the degradation rate of phenol in tap water decreased, probably because the tap water contained many minerals, which produced a certain adsorption of phenol and hindered the degradation of phenol by catalysts [12]. Nonetheless, it can degrade exceeding 96% in three kinds of water quality, meeting degradation requirements and indicating the effectiveness of Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 catalyst in real environmental contexts.

In industrial wastewater, the presence of inorganic salts, and considering that the anions in the inorganic salts may have an impact on the degradation of phenol by catalysts [70]. Hence, the influence of anions on phenol degradation was explored by adding SO4 2−, Cl−, CO3 2−, HPO4 2− and HCO3 − to the reaction system at concentrations of 0.01 mol/L and 0.1 mol/L. Fig. 9(e) and (f) display degradation rate curves. From the diagram, in the presence of CO3 2− and Cl−, degradation was inhibited with increasing concentration, likely due to CO3 2− scavenging ·OH, while Cl− may react with some Ag3PO4 and convert to AgCl during degradation [11,71], reducing degradation activity. Conversely, HCO3 − and HPO4 2−, it has a promoting effect on degradation, the aqueous solution of these two anions is weakly alkaline, while phenol is weakly acidic, which makes the pH of the reaction system neutral, which helps to improve the degradation rate of phenol. With increasing SO4 2− concentration, phenol degradation rates were promoted, likely due to the neutral pH of the Na2SO4 solution, aligning with the reaction system's pH.

The cyclic stability of the catalyst is crucial for evaluating its excellent performance of the catalyst. Illumination lasts for 40 min, with samples taken every 10 min. Following the reaction, the catalyst undergoes centrifugation, washing, and drying before cycling experiments commence. As shown in Fig. 9(g), even after four cycles, the degradation rate of Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 towards phenol remains consistently above 93% within 40 min, showing no significant reduction. This underscores the robust cycling stability of Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 composites. Furthermore, XRD characterization of the recovered catalyst post-reaction was compared with its pre-reaction state. The results are shown in Fig. 9(h) reveal no discernible change in the catalyst's crystal structure after the reaction, affirming its recyclability.

Different active substances produced during the degradation of phenol by the photocatalyst Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 have varying effects on the reaction. Capture experiments of four active substances: e-, h +,
·
O
2
−
and · OH were conducted. As depicted in Fig. 10 (a), it is evident that after the addition of AgNO3 and the introduction of N2, the degradation rate of Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 towards phenol remains nearly unchanged compared to conditions without the addition of free radical scavenger, indicating that
·
O
2
−
and e- play minimal roles in the degradation process. However, upon the addition of ammonium oxalate and tert−butanol, the degradation rates of phenol within 60 min decreased to 80% and 86%, respectively, indicating that · OH and h + were the main degradation active substances in Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 catalytic system. According to Fig. 10(b), TEMPO signal peaks display a stable triple ESR spectrum with an intensity of 1: 1: 1, detectable even in the dark. However, h + can react with TEMPO, diminishing the signal intensity, suggesting increased involvement of h + with longer illumination time [72]. Fig. 10(c) illustrates a 1: 1: 1: 1 quadruple signal peak under light, indicating a low intensity ESR spectrum of DMPO/

. No signal was detected in the dark, indicating the absence of

in the free radical capture experiment, possibly due to incomplete removal of

introduced by N2. As shown in Fig. 10(d), robust quadruple signal peaks of 1: 2: 2: 1 were observed under visible light irradiation, corresponding to the ESR spectrum of DMPO/ · OH, with no signal detected in the absence of illumination. This confirms that h + and · OH in the catalytic reaction system of Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 are the primary active substances, consistent with the results of free radical trapping experiments.

Through characterization analysis, the potential mechanism of phenol degradation in Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 composites were inferred. According to the UV–Vis DRS test results, the bandgaps of Ag3PO4 and Ni-MOF were calculated to be 2.42 eV and 3.13 eV, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 11 . A Z-scheme heterojunction was constructed between Ag3PO4 and Ni-MOF to elucidate the plausible photocatalytic mechanism. The CB of Ag3PO4 is situated at 0.47 eV, while that of Ni-MOF is at −0.78 eV. Additionally, the valence bands (VB) of Ag3PO4 and Ni-MOF are positioned at 2.89 eV and 2.35 eV, respectively. Notably, the Fermi level of Ni-MOF material is more negative than that of Ag3PO4. Combining the XPS analysis results, it can be inferred that electrons on the CB of Ag3PO4 migrate to the VB of Ni-MOF via CQDs transmission, thereby leaving behind holes with potent oxidizing capability on the valence band of Ag3PO4. Simultaneously, a significant number of electrons with strong reducing ability accumulate on the CB of Ni-MOF [73]. Serving as an efficient electron transport medium, CQDs facilitate the more efficient transfer of electrons from Ag3PO4 to Ni-MOF, thereby enhancing the performance of the Z-scheme heterojunction constructed by the system. Since the VB of Ag3PO4 is more positive than the redox potential (H2O/·OH = 2.38 V). Therefore, the holes remaining on the valence band can directly oxidize phenol to H2O and CO2. Furthermore, under visible light irradiation, they can also convert H2O to · OH, which and further reacts with phenol, achieving the objective of phenol degrading. Conversely, the CB of Ni-MOF is more negative than the redox potential of (O2/
·

= −0.046 V). Consequently, the electrons generated by light excitation ultimately converge on the CB of Ni-MOF and reduce O2 to

Ag3PO4/Ni-MOF/CQDs composites were synthesized via the in-situ deposition method, and their efficacy in degrading phenol under visible light irradiation was scrutinized. The Ag-Ni-70/CQDs-0.45 composite material exhibited an impressive degradation rate, achieving 98% degradation within just 30 min of illumination, with a degradation rate constant of 0.0620 min−1. Notably, this rate is 51.7 times higher than that of elemental CQDs and Ni-MOF, and 2.3 times higher than that of Ag3PO4. A comprehensive investigation into the influence of phenol concentration, catalyst dosage, initial pH, various water matrices and inorganic anions on degradation efficacy was conducted. The composite catalyst demonstrated remarkable stability, with the degradation rate of phenol still exceeding 93% even after undergoing four cycle experiments, indicating robust activity and stability. The formation of a Z-scheme heterojunction between Ag3PO4 and Ni-MOF, the ·OH and h + as the primary active species in the system, contributing to the catalyst's heightened activity. Several factors contributing to the enhanced catalytic activity were identified: (i) The amalgamation of Ag3PO4 and Ni-MOF alters the metal-O, fostering the strong coupling that promotes the formation of a tightly bonded interface between the semiconductors, thereby facilitating electron transport. (ii) Introduction of CQDs serves as an electron transport medium, expediting charge transfer rates while inhibiting the recombination of electron-hole pairs, thereby enhancing the catalyst's degradation performance.
